title: Replacing an internal stopcock
subtitle: Swap out a worn or leaking mains shut-off valve on the rising main
excerpt: How to replace a failed or stiff internal stopcock on the rising main: isolating the supply, draining the pipe, removing the old valve, fitting the new one with PTFE tape, and restoring the water.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Gilling and Parry, Plumbing for Dummies.

glossaryTerms.definition: The isolation valve fitted on the cold water rising main inside the property, usually under the kitchen sink, in a utility room, or below a ground-floor cupboard. Turning it clockwise shuts off the cold water supply to the whole property. Also called the main service valve or consumer stop valve.

A quarter-turn or screwdriver-slot isolation valve fitted on a branch pipe close to an appliance. Distinct from the full-bore stopcock on the rising main; a service valve isolates a single appliance without affecting the rest of the supply.

content.text: that weeps around the gland or refuses to turn fully is a risk rather than a cosmetic problem. A failed stopcock leaves the property unable to isolate its water supply in an emergency. The replacement is a straightforward pipe joint, but it requires isolating the supply at the external boundary stop valve before work can begin.

Locate the external boundary stop valve (under a cover in the footpath or front garden) before starting. If this valve is also stuck or inaccessible, contact the water company before touching the internal stopcock.

Turn off the external boundary stop valve clockwise. Open the kitchen cold tap fully to drain the rising main. Flush the toilet once to confirm the supply is off. Place towels or a small container under the work area; residual water will run from the pipe when it is cut.

Note the flow direction arrow on the existing stopcock body before removing it. Undo the compression nuts at both ends using the adjustable spanner while counter-holding the valve body with the pipe wrench to avoid twisting the pipe. If the pipe is corroded and the nut will not free, cut the pipe on each side of the valve with the pipe cutter, leaving enough pipe to fit new fittings.

Clean both pipe ends with the emery cloth until the copper is bright and free of scale. Check the pipe ends are square with the pipe cutter. Slide new compression nuts and olives onto the pipe before fitting the new stopcock.

A quarter-turn ceramic-disc stopcock is the modern replacement for a traditional brass gate-valve stopcock; it will not seize. Fit with the flow arrow pointing in the direction of flow (away from the boundary). A

is not a substitute for the rising-main stopcock; fit the correct full-bore type. Tighten compression nuts finger-tight plus a quarter turn with the spanner; no PTFE tape is needed on compression olives, but apply five wraps of PTFE tape if the end connections are threaded rather than compression.

Open the new stopcock fully (lever inline with the pipe). Turn the external boundary valve back on slowly. Check both compression joints for drips while the supply is restored. If a joint weeps, tighten by a further eighth-turn; avoid over-tightening, which splits the olive.

attrs.cause: The olive has not been compressed evenly against the pipe, or the pipe end is not square.
attrs.fix: Turn off supply, cut back the pipe to expose fresh copper, fit a new olive, and re-make the joint.

attrs.symptom: Pipe too short to reach the new valve after cutting out the old one
attrs.cause: The old valve fittings were different dimensions and the cut removed too much pipe.
attrs.fix: Add a short push-fit extension piece to bridge the gap before fitting the new valve.
